does: This paper identifies and systematizes reproducibility problems in agent research by conducting a structured audit of 50 training and evaluation repositories. The authors find that none report key metadata like failed or skipped runs alongside headline scores, and document 37 cases where different reporting rules can dramatically alter reported metrics for identical underlying evidence. In response, they introduce rollout cards: publication bundles that preserve complete rollout records and explicitly declare the views, reporting rules, and data-filtering decisions used to generate reported scores. Rather than treating reported scores as the unit of reproducibility, the work elevates rollout records as the fundamental artifact for transparency and verification in agent research.
connects: This work extends longstanding reproducibility concerns in machine learning and reinforcement learning into the emerging domain of agent research. It builds on prior work documenting reporting issues and metric manipulation in ML—such as problems with cherry-picked results, undocumented hyperparameter sweeps, and selective reporting—and applies systematic auditing methodology to the new context of agentic systems where evaluation complexity creates additional opacity. The paper responds to growing recognition that standard experimental practices from supervised learning are insufficient for agent evaluation, where trajectory-level evidence and multiple possible score aggregations can yield inconsistent conclusions from the same underlying behavior.
enables: By establishing rollout cards as a reproducibility standard, this work enables future agent research to build on transparent, inspectable evidence rather than opaque aggregated scores. It creates infrastructure for auditing published claims, detecting reporting rule changes across papers, and comparing systems fairly when using the same rollout records but different metrics. The standardized format facilitates meta-analyses that examine how reporting choices affect conclusions, supports development of better evaluation practices, and encourages adoption of audit trails in agent benchmarks. Longer term, rollout cards could become a requirement for publication in agent research venues, fostering institutional change toward reproducibility similar to data-sharing mandates in genomics and psychology.
